reviews.review_text:
A cute, relaxing cartoon that secretly requires a Master's degree in thermodynamics.

Oxygen Not Included is arguably the greatest Trojan Horse in modern gaming. On the surface, it looks like a delightful, casual colony sim where adorable little animated clones run around a colorful asteroid. It lulls you into a false sense of security with its charming, bubbly aesthetic and quirky animations. But do not be fooled for a second—beneath that cute exterior lies one of the most intricate, punishing, and deeply complex survival management games ever created. You think you're signing up for a chill time, and suddenly you are desperately trying to calculate fluid dynamics just to stop your base from filling with toxic gas.

The way the gameplay scales is both terrifying and brilliantly addictive. Your first hour is wonderfully simple: dig a hole, build an outhouse, and find some algae to breathe. Easy, right? Cut to ten hours later, and your entire colony is collapsing because your clones accidentally peed in the fresh water supply, the carbon dioxide has pooled in the sleeping quarters, and your makeshift cooling system just boiled your only farmable food. The game throws layer upon layer of scientific mechanics at you—from plumbing and power grids to disease control and temperature management. It is the ultimate "time sink" game; you will blink, realize it's 3:00 AM, and you are still staring at your screen trying to perfectly route a single ventilation pipe.

Despite how incredibly fast things can spiral out of control, it is a phenomenally fun and rewarding experience. The genius of the game is that every catastrophic failure is entirely your fault, but it’s also a learning opportunity that makes you want to immediately start a new colony and do it better. If you love games that let you tinker, optimize, and completely lose track of reality for hours on end, this is an absolute masterpiece. Just be prepared to spend an embarrassing amount of time Googling how real-world plumbing works.

10/10 — Everything is fine until the air turns yellow.

A Masterpiece of Controlled Chaos and Thermodynamics

The Good
Unmatched Engineering Depth: This isn't just a survival game; it's a simulation of physics, gasses, liquids, and thermodynamics. You will design functional plumbing, automated power grids, and complex gas filtering systems.

Adorably Dumb Colony Members: Your "Duplicants" are full of personality. They stress-vomit, cry, binge-eat, and perform hilarious animations, making you genuinely care about keeping them alive.

Infinite Replayability: No two asteroids are the same. Procedural generation combined with various biomes, geysers, and space destinations ensures every playthrough offers a unique engineering puzzle.

Rewarding Automation: Hooking up logic gates, sensors, and shipping rails to make a fully automated, self-sustaining base feels like an incredible achievement.

The Spaced Out! DLC: The expansion seamlessly integrates rocketry and multi-asteroid management, effectively doubling the game's complexity and scope.

The Bad
The Lethal "Noob" Traps: The game hides its deadliest threats. Your base might look fine on Cycle 50, but silent killers like trapped heat, carbon dioxide buildup, or running out of algae will slowly destroy your colony 30 cycles later.

FPS Death (Endgame Performance): Once your base spans the entire map with thousands of pipes, wires, and pathfinding calculations, the game's frame rate can drop significantly on mid-range PCs.

Verdict: 11 / 10
Oxygen Not Included is a phenomenal, brutally challenging colony simulator that demands your full attention. It rewards logic, planning, and problem-solving like no other game on the market. If you enjoy games like Factorio or RimWorld, buy this immediately—just prepare to keep a calculator next to your keyboard.
Addictive and cozy, but also hurts my brain sometimes....

ONI was pretty intimidating to me but I finally gave it a shot and I am HOOKED.

I ended up playing 11 hours straight my first session and have 38 hours in 3 days...

Biggest advice for new players: It's okay to fail. Let your first colony be the wake up call, the 2nd the learner, and the third an actual attempt. You may get further than expected and even if you learn lessons the hard way, you'll never forget them.
Poor meep fell into the water container, pissed himself. Then died 10/10 game

Don't let the cute art style and the daft little dupes fool you. This isn't a chill builder, it's a thermodynamics PhD wearing a colony sim as a disguise. The title's a bit of a joke really, oxygen is the least of your worries once you're knee deep in heat management, gas distribution and a pipe network that's turned into spaghetti.

What Klei have nailed is consistency. Gases, liquids, heat, power and automation all run on rules that stay the same no matter how chaotic the colony gets, so when something breaks it's always your fault, never the game cheating you. That's what makes solving it so satisfying. The first time a Self-Powered Oxygen Machine stabilises your whole base you feel like an actual engineer.

It's deep. Properly deep. You're juggling temperature, oxygen, hunger, stress and comfort all at once, and every fix spawns three new problems. The flip side is the learning curve. The in-game teaching is thin, so expect to lose a few colonies before it clicks, and some folk bounce off it for that exact reason.

Verdict: If you like colony builders with real systems mastery rather than hand-holding, this is one of the best around. Sitting at 97/100 across 135,000+ Steam reviews for good reason. 9/10, would suffocate my dupes again.
This is one of the most compelling games ever made.  Just fantastic.  Replayable as anything I've seen.
kinda good, almost 500hrs in 2 months just understanding the basics but it was totally worth it
After investing some time into this game, I suppose I have no choice but to say I like it.
tried this game numerous times and really couldn't wrap my head around it. ended up just finding it frustrating, not very intuitive, and repetitive. probably just a me thing.
I don't know if i'd be able to look at someone the same way if they said they dont like at least one aspect of this game...
All the love and the details from the developer you can see in every duplicant step. Is not easy to beat, but you will certainly enjoy it.

your guys spawn
must expand
underground. everything dirt
expand too far, hit slime
everyone dying of slimelung
dies of starvation instead because they dug themselves into a dumb spot
everyone dead.
10/10 would kill everyone via starvation and suffocation again
My brain hurts after playing this game. You really need to be planning everything 20 days in advance before you even begin to add more duplicates. I feel more organized after playing!
This is one of my favorite games that I keep coming back to. Love the artstyle, and the elaborate colony building with many factors that need balancing.

You will have fun, then you may cry, then you will have even more fun because you learned something new whether you wanted to or not :D

The learning curve is a roller coaster, but that's honestly what I enjoy about the game. It's a great experience for people who enjoy learning new things. Even if you don't, and you're anything like I was, you'll still come back to try again just for the challenge of it until you come to love it anyways. This game is an addicting loop of "ooo, what if I tried this?" I spent about a year away from the game, and it still weaseled a thought into my head of trying something different with the colony... Now I just racked up "103.1hrs in the last two weeks......" I love it.

The community is AMAZING. There are a gazillion resources for learning (not a bad thing!), YouTube guides, tutorials, reddit threads, and you'll likely even come across a forum chain with the devs at some point, helping players with niche, OS-specific bugs. AHemLMDEthankyouKlei. The game is VERY Linux friendly.

"PhD in Thermodynamics preferred, but not required for this position."

I assume if you're reading this, you're considering getting the game. If so, I highly encourage you to at least try it out. You'll likely find yourself hooked, then confused, then lost, then upside down, and then hooked again by the absolute thrill of "Ha! I figured it out!" You don't need to min/max, but you can. You don't need to cookie-cutter copy anyone else's designs for certain systems, but you definitely can, and it's encouraged if you need to. If you make something you haven't seen anywhere else, share it with the community! or don't, I'm not your parent.

The UI may be ever so slightly different from some videos you've watched (ie; Markiplier and some Magnet videos) when you play, but the core game mechanics are largely the same, save for the additions. You don't need the DLC to enjoy the game, but some of them are certainly worth looking at. Just play the game first, and if it's somehow not enough for you, go ham lol

Thank you for reading my ramblings, I'm just a fan of a niche internet game, doing what I can to help ;P
I will honestly be shocked if this isn't the first game I log 1,000hrs in (on Steam). I will edit this review if I get there.

I keep dropping ONI in order to play other games, and I think it's because I'm not clever enough to make things work really well/efficiently without looking online for things others have done. With that said, I come back to it again and again because it's extremely well made, very fun, and...cheerful is the best word I can think of. I love watching my little dupes going about their business. If you either don't mind using ideas from other players or can keep from getting caught up in wanting things to be PERFECT then you'll probably stick with this game longer at the outset. Either way, it's easy to dump a ton of time into ONI and be happy to do it.
The kind of game that will make your night evaporate in the best way.
I love the sandbox mode of it. I like the more technical games and this checks all my boxes. Each colony brings a new challenge and the DLC's are a blast.

176 hours in and I'm still learning new ways to accidentally kill my colony.

Oxygen Not Included looks cute until you realize you're managing oxygen production, food supply, plumbing, power generation, heat transfer, gas dynamics, and the consequences of every bad decision you've made over the last 50 cycles.

The game does a great job of turning small problems into larger, more interesting problems. Fix the oxygen issue and now you have a heat issue. Fix the heat issue and now your power grid is overloaded. Fix that and somehow your duplicants are trapped behind a door you automated yourself.

If you enjoy solving problems, optimizing systems, and occasionally discovering that your entire base is one broken pipe away from collapse, you'll probably love it.

176 hours played. Still haven't built the perfect colony.
oops forgot to press like, just like how I forgot to build an outhouse
At first the game was not so interesting, however, once you begin to understand the mechanics and the objective of the game, it becomes super addicting and fun.

Oxygen Not Included is one of the most interesting creative solving games I've ever played.

Besides standard worker/production gameplay, the physics system with heat and phase transitions making for some very interesint play.

Be warned that there is a pretty large learning curve and it can take 50-100 hours of gameplay to start to get a handle of things and unlock the mechanics.

Whats even better is theres no game quite like it and it doesnt have crazy prices, you dont even need the dlc to play for hundreds of hours, theres so much content to explore and learn about.

I wake up thinking about ventilation and plumbing systems.

Great game if you like survival and have a engineers heart and problem solving sprit.

This is probably one of the best colony simulator games I've played. Unlike games such as Rimworld, Oxygen Not Included puts an increased emphasis on thermodynamics and simple physical liquids.

Duplicants are hilarious creatures. I vividly remember watching Nesbitt angrily lash out on a Manual Generator while another Duplicant repaired it at the same rate it was being destroyed. Obviously, they were striking friendly small talk with each other while doing this.

I mostly play the sandbox gamemode simply because I don't have the patience to do the survival mode. From experience, you die, get better, die later, get better, and then die even later, and so on.

Art is amazing. Soundtrack is incredible. Community is nice, but this game is hard to stop playing once you start.
This is very creative strategy game play with unique art style i love. I strongly recommend this game to everyone who love strategic and management game.
pretty good, rog ally got to like cycle 200 before it start to lag bad,
This is the most rage-inducing, blood boiling, aggravating, and moronic game I have ever played. I love it. 10/10

Base game is outstandingly polished product at this point and the expansions are worth every penny. The expansions dont just add more AFTER basegame, they add things that you can enjoy and improves right out the gate. Get em all. Play in sequence. If your this far down the reviews its beacuse you analyze things way to much and this game is gonna be right up your alley. Im strict with my spending and this game just keeps giving bang for its buck years later. 10/10, would buy again.

This is an actual masterpiece. Brought to us by the creators of the "Don't Starve" series. 
Don't be fooled by the simple graphics and playful attitude it presents. The game does force you to think properly about your actions. And has a lot more complexity than may seem at first.
A lot is based on learning to understand it's nature as a closed system game, managing complex resource loops while trying to stay ahead of entropy, and understand that every action has consequences that tilt the balance. And it will force back towards balance whether you like it or not. 
My advice? Play a few games and accept that you will probably fail, but at least learn something.
Pretty fun and challenging to keep the dupes alive. Worth every penny!
amazing. but prepare for a time of your life. this game is easy to play but hard to master. i love every bet of it.

It gets a bit too complicated later on, but the early and mid game is a very fun gameplay loop.

Oxygen Not Included is the one of the most satisfying logistical chaos simulator I’ve ever played.  
If you love systems, efficiency, and watching a colony go from “barely breathing” to “fully automated space‑age industrial utopia,” this game delivers in every way.

You start with three clueless duplicants and a pocket of breathable air. From there, it becomes a beautifully complex puzzle of pipes, gases, heat, power grids, automation, and resource loops. Every system connects to another system, and every decision matters. It’s like managing a tiny space factory where physics is real and consequences are immediate.

The best part?  
When your base finally runs smoothly, it feels earned. You didn’t luck into stability—you engineered it.

👍 What Makes It Great
Deep logistics: Gas flow, liquid routing, heat transfer, automation—everything behaves predictably and rewards smart planning.

Incredible transparency: Overlays for oxygen, heat, germs, power, stress… diagnosing problems feels like running a real control room.

Automation heaven: Sensors, switches, smart pumps, conveyor rails—your base becomes a self-regulating machine.

Long-term payoff: Early planning actually matters. A well-designed pipe layout at cycle 20 still pays dividends at cycle 500.

Endgame depth: Rockets, interplanetary supply chains, rare materials—your logistics brain never stops getting fed.

😂 Also Worth Noting
You will accidentally flood your base.
You will cook your crops.
You will forget to vent hydrogen and turn your colony into a bomb.
And you’ll love every second of fixing it.

⭐ Final Verdict
If you enjoy logistics, engineering puzzles, or watching a plan come together, Oxygen Not Included is a masterpiece. It’s the kind of game where you say “just one more cycle” and suddenly it’s 3 AM.

Klei quirky (90's dark vibe throwback) environment exploitation simulator. Every time you solve a problem a new one presents. Art style holds up. If you like turning chaos into order this scratches the itch but it can also be frustrating as hell when you think you are on track and a new problem sends your base into a death spiral you now need to replay to figure out.

My only complaint is that you have to rebuild structures over and over. What we need is a copy/paste blueprint function, like Factorio offers.

This isnt my first time playing, this is just my first time owning this. But theres a point where you dont know what you need to do, and when you hit that point just focus on upgrading decore and automating basic needs. everything else will come with it.

Oxygen Not Included Review: 10/10

Spent five hours designing a brilliant, complex plumbing system to perfectly filter our drinking water. One of my colonists immediately got stressed, peed in the fresh water reservoir, and now the entire base has food poisoning and is suffocating in a cloud of unbreathable gas.

    Pros: Incredibly deep simulation, rewarding base-building, and you essentially earn an honorary degree in thermodynamic engineering and fluid dynamics just to keep the lights on.

    Cons: Realizing too late that the title is literally an immediate, life-threatening problem, and your little guys will actively find the absolute dumbest ways to trap themselves inside a wall they just built.

Highly recommend if you want to experience the joy of watching a thriving, highly technical society completely collapse because you forgot to build an outhouse!
Awesome, but please make an official, gas tight, air lock part.

Tips and tricks #57

Slimelung germs perish at a temperature below 10 °C quite quickly. 

Tips and tricks #89

Abyssalite is a natural thermal insulator. In-game this means that Abyssalite at minus degrees will cool down gas and liquid passing by in the standard tiles, especially when radiant pipe is used. And when combining it with insulation pipe after the cooling segment, i.e. freezing air can be brought to a freezer room.
